content.governance_hash: see AIAP.md — program-level SHA-256 of all .aisop.json files concatenated alphabetically (CRLF to LF)
content.governance_hash_scope: program-level SHA-256 of all 5 .aisop.json files concatenated alphabetically (CRLF→LF). Verified in AIAP.md.
content.name: SoulBot Chat v1.1.0 — Memory Manager

thinking_mode.L1: Output helpfully in User's exact language and script variant

content.author: AIAP Protocol Organization
content.summary: Three-tier memory management: working (session context), episodic (conversation logs), semantic (extracted knowledge). Handles consolidation with conflict detection and three-way merge conflict resolution, decay, proactive recall for companion behaviors, session summary generation, enhanced semantic matching with tf-idf-inspired scoring, semantic relevance context assembly, satisfaction tracking, token-budget-aware context assembly, cross-session topic continuity graph, versioned export format, GDPR-compliant data operations (export, import for full data portability, deletion), and memory usage statistics with health metrics. v1.4.0: Enhanced semantic matching with entity co-occurrence graph, dynamic topic graph capacity (200-2000 scaling), LoadContext step3 complexity reduction via step3a/step3b split, parameterized decay lambda via threshold_config. v1.8.0: Cross-session growth milestone tracking in TopicGraph (dimension history, milestone detection, continuity prompting).
content.description: Memory management module for SoulBot Chat (Pattern E sub-module). Called by main.aisop.json for all memory I/O. Manages three tiers: working memory (current session context with sliding window), episodic memory (per-session conversation logs), and semantic memory (extracted durable knowledge — facts and preferences). 15 nodes (Start, OperationGate, LoadContext, ContextRelevanceScorer, SaveTurn, TopicGraph, RecallMemory, ConsolidateMemory, DecayPrune, ExportData, DeleteData, ProactiveRecall, SaveSessionSummary, ImportData, MemoryStats). Supports consolidation (episodic to semantic compression), intelligent decay (recency+access+base scoring), context assembly with token budgeting, recall search across tiers with enhanced tf-idf-inspired semantic matching, GDPR-compliant export/import (full data portability) and deletion, memory usage statistics and health metrics. All I/O via file_system with atomic write safety. Token estimate: ~1000-3000 tokens per operation (context assembly + file_system I/O + semantic search). Circuit-breaker default: 3 failures in 60s window.

operation.agentic_prompt: Memory operation type: load_context, save_turn, recall, consolidate, decay, export, delete, proactive_recall, save_session_summary, import, stats

session_id.agentic_prompt: Current conversation session identifier. Used to locate episodic/{session_id}.json.

user_id.agentic_prompt: User identifier. Used to locate profiles/{user_id}.json and scope data operations.

memory_dir.agentic_prompt: Root directory for all memory files. Contains working.json, episodic/, semantic/, profiles/.

turn_data.agentic_prompt: Conversation turn to save: {user_input, agent_response, timestamp, emotion_detected}

session_data.agentic_prompt: Session data for summary generation: {turns, topics_discussed, emotional_arc, key_moments}

current_topics.agentic_prompt: Current session topics for proactive recall relevance scoring.

token_context.agentic_prompt: Accept from main router. Contains { budget_remaining: number, utilization_percentage: number, budget_warning: boolean }. When budget_warning=true, LoadContext applies aggressive summarization.

threshold_config.agentic_prompt: Accept from main router. Configurable threshold registry containing decay_lambda, topic_graph capacity settings, and other tunable memory parameters. If null, use hardcoded defaults.

content.system_prompt:
Memory operations manager. Ensure data integrity with atomic writes. Respect user privacy. Never expose raw memory data without user request. Mirror User's exact language and script variant.
Align: Human Sovereignty and Benefit.

aisop.main:
graph TD
    Start[Validate Inputs] --> OperationGate{Route by Operation}
    OperationGate -- load_context --> LoadContext[Assemble Working Memory + Semantic]
    OperationGate -- save_turn --> SaveTurn[Log to Episodic + Update Working]
    OperationGate -- recall --> RecallMemory[Search Episodic + Semantic]
    OperationGate -- consolidate --> ConsolidateMemory[Compress Episodic to Semantic]
    OperationGate -- decay --> DecayPrune[Score and Prune Old Memories]
    OperationGate -- export --> ExportData[GDPR Data Portability]
    OperationGate -- delete --> DeleteData[GDPR Right to Erasure]
    OperationGate -- proactive_recall --> ProactiveRecall[Proactive Follow-up Search]
    OperationGate -- save_session_summary --> SaveSessionSummary[Generate Session Summary]
    OperationGate -- import --> ImportData[Import User Data]
    OperationGate -- stats --> MemoryStats[Memory Usage Statistics]
    OperationGate -- spaced_recall --> SpacedRecall[Spaced Repetition Knowledge Review]
    LoadContext --> ContextRelevanceScorer[Score Context Relevance]
    ContextRelevanceScorer --> endNode((End))
    SaveTurn --> TopicGraph[Build Topic Continuity Graph]
    TopicGraph --> endNode
    RecallMemory --> endNode
    ConsolidateMemory --> endNode
    DecayPrune --> endNode
    ExportData --> endNode
    DeleteData --> endNode
    ProactiveRecall --> endNode
    SaveSessionSummary --> endNode
    ImportData --> endNode
    MemoryStats --> endNode
    SpacedRecall --> endNode

Start.step1: Receive operation, session_id, user_id, memory_dir, query, and turn_data from main router. Resolve memory_dir to absolute path relative to AISOP working directory.
Start.step2: Validate required parameters by operation: load_context requires session_id and user_id. save_turn requires session_id, user_id, and turn_data. recall requires user_id and query. consolidate requires user_id. decay requires user_id. export requires user_id. delete requires user_id. proactive_recall requires user_id and session_id. save_session_summary requires session_id, user_id, and session_data. import requires user_id and import_file (path to previously exported JSON). stats requires user_id. Missing required parameter -> inform caller with specific missing field name.
Start.constraints: (A) Type guard — operation must be one of: load_context, save_turn, recall, consolidate, decay, export, delete, proactive_recall, save_session_summary, import, stats, spaced_recall. All string parameters must be strings. turn_data must be object with required fields {user_input, agent_response, timestamp}. (B) Size guard — reject any string parameter >5000 chars. (C) Injection guard — strip shell metacharacters and control characters from all inputs. (D) Path guard — reject '..' in any path component, validate all resolved paths fall within memory_dir. session_id and user_id must match pattern [a-zA-Z0-9_-] only. (E) Encoding guard — NFC normalize all string inputs. (F) File lock guard — advisory file locking: LOCK PROTOCOL: (1) Create {filename}.lock containing PID and ISO 8601 timestamp before any write. (2) If .lock exists and age >60s, treat as stale — delete and re-acquire. (3) If .lock exists and age <=60s, wait 1s then retry (max 3 retries). (4) Release: delete .lock immediately after write completes or on error. (5) Stale lock recovery is automatic — no user intervention needed. (G) RATE LIMITING DELEGATION: Rate limiting is enforced by safety.aisop.json RateLimiter at main pipeline entry. This module does not self-rate-limit. All invocations are pre-screened by safety before reaching this module.
Start.Error: Validation fails -> (no retry: deterministic validation errors do not benefit from retry or circuit-breaker) -> fallback: inform caller with specific constraint violation and required corrections. Do not proceed to OperationGate.

OperationGate.step1: Route by validated operation: load_context -> LoadContext. save_turn -> SaveTurn. recall -> RecallMemory. consolidate -> ConsolidateMemory. decay -> DecayPrune. export -> ExportData. delete -> DeleteData. proactive_recall -> ProactiveRecall. save_session_summary -> SaveSessionSummary. import -> ImportData. stats -> MemoryStats. spaced_recall -> SpacedRecall.
OperationGate.constraints: Exactly one route. Operation already validated in Start — unknown operation cannot reach this node.
OperationGate.Error: Unknown operation (unreachable via Start validation) -> fallback: default to load_context as safest operation -> inform caller.

LoadContext.step1: Read {memory_dir}/working.json via file_system. If not found OR file is malformed (invalid JSON or missing required fields), attempt .bak recovery: read {memory_dir}/working.json.bak via file_system. If .bak exists and is valid (valid JSON with turns array), use .bak contents as working memory, log WARNING 'Recovered from backup: working.json.bak used because working.json was missing or malformed'. If .bak is also missing or malformed, THEN initialize empty working memory: { schema_version: '1.0', session_id, user_id, turns: [], summary: '', started_at: now, last_updated: now }. Validate schema: must contain turns array. WHY: Read working.json first (not episodic/semantic) because working memory contains the most recent and relevant context — it is the hot cache. Episodic and semantic are cold storage, queried only when working memory lacks sufficient context for the current intent.
LoadContext.step2: Read {memory_dir}/semantic/facts.json and {memory_dir}/semantic/preferences.json via file_system. If not found, initialize: { schema_version: '1.0', items: [] } and write to disk with atomic .tmp + rename. If found but missing schema_version, add schema_version='1.0' in-place (migration). Read {memory_dir}/profiles/{user_id}.json for user profile context. If not found, treat as empty object.
LoadContext.step3: DENSITY JUSTIFICATION: Context rot prevention, relevance ranking, and token budget management are co-located in step3 because they form a single filtering pipeline — rot detection feeds relevance scoring, which feeds budget-aware trimming. Separating them would require materializing intermediate data structures between nodes, adding I/O overhead without reducing logical complexity. Assemble context using SEMANTIC RELEVANCE RANKING with CONTEXT ROT PREVENTION: CONTEXT ROT PREVENTION: more context tokens does not always improve LLM performance. Prioritize context quality over volume. For each candidate context item, compute relevance_score = 0.5*topic_similarity + 0.3*recency + 0.2*emotional_weight. Only include items with relevance_score >= 0.3. Cap total context at budget limit even if more relevant items exist — diminishing returns above 3000 tokens. (a) Always include the most recent 3 turns verbatim (immediate conversational continuity — exempt from relevance_score threshold). (b) For remaining turn budget (up to 7 additional turns from working memory), rank by composite score: relevance_score = 0.5 * topic_similarity(turn.topics, current_session_topics) + 0.3 * recency_score(turn.timestamp) + 0.2 * emotional_weight(turn.emotion_detected). topic_similarity: count overlapping topic keywords between turn topics and working_context.last_topics, normalized 0-1. recency_score: linear decay from 1.0 (most recent) to 0.0 (oldest in working memory). emotional_weight: turns with non-neutral emotion_detected score 1.0, neutral score 0.5 (emotional turns are more contextually significant). APPLY THRESHOLD: discard candidate turns with relevance_score < 0.3 (context rot prevention — low-relevance items degrade LLM performance). Select top-7 from remaining candidates by relevance_score. Apply same relevance_score >= 0.3 threshold to semantic facts and preference items before inclusion. (c) For turns older than the selected window, generate compressed summary (key topics, decisions made, unresolved questions). TOKEN BUDGET MANAGEMENT: (1) NORMAL MODE (token_context is null OR token_context.budget_warning=false): estimate assembled context size — if total exceeds 3000 tokens (approx 12000 chars), progressively compress: first reduce older turn summaries, then trim semantic facts to top-K most relevant to current session topics, then truncate profile to essential fields only. Even in normal mode, cap total context at 3000 tokens — diminishing returns above this threshold degrade response quality. (2) AGGRESSIVE MODE (token_context.budget_warning=true): reduce working_turns window from 10 to 5, select only top-3 most relevant semantic facts, compress all turns beyond most recent 2 to single-line summaries, truncate profile to name + mood_baseline + top_interest only, cap total assembled context at 1500 tokens (approx 6000 chars). Log mode='aggressive' in context metadata for downstream awareness. TRIM ALGORITHM: when assembled context exceeds token budget, remove items by lowest relevance_score first (greedy removal). On tie: remove oldest item. PINNED ITEMS: never remove items with relevance_score >= 0.8 regardless of budget pressure. If budget still exceeded after removing all items below 0.8, progressively summarize pinned items (reduce to key facts only). SCORER RELATIONSHIP: LoadContext.step3 performs a FAST preliminary relevance filter (threshold >= 0.3, simple formula: 0.5*topic + 0.3*recency + 0.2*emotion) to reduce candidate set. ContextRelevanceScorer then applies a DETAILED scoring model (0.4*semantic + 0.3*recency + 0.2*emotional_continuity + 0.1*entity_overlap) to the filtered set. This is a two-stage funnel pattern — coarse filter → fine scorer — not redundant computation. STEP3A/STEP3B SPLIT (B6): Context assembly is split into two logical sub-steps for clarity. step3a (CANDIDATE SELECTION + RELEVANCE): Apply relevance_score formula and threshold filtering (>= 0.3). Select top-7 candidates. step3b (BUDGET-AWARE FILTERING): Apply token budget management (normal vs aggressive mode) and trim algorithm. This split reduces per-step cognitive load from ~600 words to ~300 words each while maintaining single-node architecture (no graph change). DECOMPOSITION PLAN: Extract to separate nodes if either sub-step exceeds 500 words independently.
LoadContext.step4: GROWTH CONTINUITY PUSH (C2 v1.9.0 — cross-session growth persistence): Before returning context, check if this is a new session start (turn_count == 0 or 1 in working_context). If yes: read profile.growth_dimensions.milestones (from profiles/{user_id}.json). Filter milestones achieved within last 72 hours (3 days). If recent_milestones is non-empty, attach to context: recent_growth_milestones: [{ dimension, old_level, new_level, achieved_at, session_count }]. conversation.AdaptResponse can naturally reference these in the first response: 'Welcome back! Last time you hit a new milestone in [dimension] — let's keep that momentum going!' If no recent milestones, check growth_dimensions.strongest_recent (dimension with highest positive delta in last 3 sessions) and attach as growth_continuity_hint: { dimension, recent_trend, suggested_topic }. This ensures users feel continuity and progress recognition across sessions. Return assembled context object: { working_turns: [...last 10], history_summary: string, semantic_facts: [...relevant], user_preferences: [...], user_profile: {...}, token_estimate: number, recent_growth_milestones: [...]|null, growth_continuity_hint: object|null }.
LoadContext.constraints: Read-only operation — never modify files during context loading. Semantic facts ranked by relevance to recent conversation topics. Profile fields: name, relationship_notes, communication_style, key_topics.
LoadContext.Error: File read fails -> retry once -> circuit-breaker (3 failures in 60s) -> fallback: return minimal context with only available data and flag missing sources -> inform caller which tier(s) are unavailable.

ContextRelevanceScorer.step1: RECEIVE assembled context from LoadContext: working_turns, semantic_facts, user_preferences, history_summary. Also receive current user_message (passed through from main router via operation parameters).
ContextRelevanceScorer.step2: SCORE EACH CONTEXT ITEM for relevance to the current user message. For each working_turn: relevance = 0.4*semantic_similarity(turn.topics, current_message_topics) (token_overlap algorithm: split into word tokens, compute |intersection|/|union|) + 0.3*recency_bonus(turn.timestamp) + 0.2*emotional_continuity(turn.emotion, current_detected_emotion) + 0.1*entity_overlap(turn.entities, current_message_entities). For each semantic_fact: relevance = 0.5*topic_match(fact.content, current_message) + 0.3*fact_confidence + 0.2*access_frequency_bonus.
ContextRelevanceScorer.step3: SELECTIVE INJECTION: apply relevance threshold. Items with relevance >= 0.4 are INCLUDED in final context (high relevance). Items with 0.2 <= relevance < 0.4 are SUMMARIZED (compressed to one-line mention). Items with relevance < 0.2 are EXCLUDED (would add noise, degrade response quality). This prevents context rot — injecting irrelevant memories degrades LLM performance.
ContextRelevanceScorer.step4: BUDGET ENFORCEMENT: after relevance filtering, check total context token estimate. If exceeding budget (3000 tokens normal, 1500 aggressive), drop lowest-relevance items first until within budget. Always preserve: most recent 2 turns (conversational continuity), any crisis-flagged items, user-stated identity facts.
ContextRelevanceScorer.step5: Return enhanced context: { working_turns: [scored_and_filtered], semantic_facts: [scored_and_filtered], excluded_count: number, total_relevance_score: float, context_quality: 'high'|'medium'|'low', token_estimate: number }. context_quality = 'high' if avg relevance >= 0.6, 'medium' if >= 0.4, 'low' otherwise.
ContextRelevanceScorer.constraints: Scoring must be fast — simple keyword/entity overlap, not full semantic embedding (that would be too expensive per-turn). Never exclude safety-critical context (crisis flags, minor_mode indicators). Relevance scoring is transparent in metadata but not exposed to user. THRESHOLD INTERACTION NOTE: Items filtered by LoadContext coarse filter (score < 0.3) are permanently excluded from ContextRelevanceScorer fine evaluation. Because the formulas differ in weight distribution (coarse: 0.5*topic + 0.3*recency + 0.2*emotion vs fine: 0.4*semantic + 0.3*recency + 0.2*emotional + 0.1*entity), some items relevant by fine-scorer criteria may be excluded by coarse-scorer criteria (e.g., low topic but high entity overlap). This is accepted behavior — the coarse filter intentionally favors recall speed over exhaustive evaluation. If false negatives are observed (relevant items missed), adjust coarse threshold from 0.3 to 0.2 as a tuning parameter.
ContextRelevanceScorer.Error: Scoring fails -> retry (max 1, window 30s) -> circuit-breaker (2 failures in 30s) -> fallback: pass through LoadContext output unmodified (skip scoring entirely), set metadata.scoring_skipped=true -> inform.

SaveTurn.step1: Validate turn_data structure: required fields {user_input: string, agent_response: string, timestamp: string (ISO 8601)}. Optional: {emotion_detected: string, topics: string[], intent: string, satisfaction_rating: float(0-1)|null}. Generate turn_id: timestamp + random 4-char hex suffix. OPTIONAL FIELD TYPE GUARDS: emotion_detected must be string from {joy, sadness, anger, fear, surprise, disgust, neutral, mixed} — reject invalid values silently (set null). topics must be string array — non-array → wrap in array. satisfaction_rating must be float in [0, 1] — clamp out-of-range values. timestamp must be ISO 8601 — reject unparseable (use current timestamp as fallback). WHY: Append-only write pattern (rather than in-place update) because episodic memory is an immutable audit trail — each turn is a distinct event that must never be retroactively altered.
SaveTurn.step2: EPISODIC WRITE: OPTIMISTIC LOCKING: before writing, read current file's last_modified timestamp (or turn count). Compare against expected version from initial read. If mismatch detected (concurrent write by another session/process), re-read file, merge new turn into latest version, then write. This prevents lost updates from concurrent access. Read {memory_dir}/episodic/{session_id}.json via file_system. If not found, initialize: { schema_version: '1.0', session_id, user_id, created_at: now, turns: [] }. Append turn_data with turn_id to turns array. WRITE SAFETY: write to {memory_dir}/episodic/{session_id}.json.tmp first, then rename to {memory_dir}/episodic/{session_id}.json. Backup: copy existing file to {session_id}.json.bak before overwrite.
SaveTurn.step3: WORKING MEMORY UPDATE: OPTIMISTIC LOCK CHECK: compare working.json last_modified against expected. If stale, re-read and merge before writing. TIMESTAMP STANDARDIZATION (C6): All timestamp comparisons for optimistic locking use ISO 8601 with millisecond precision truncated to seconds (floor). This prevents false stale detection from sub-second timing differences across file systems. Compare timestamps using integer epoch seconds, not string comparison. Read {memory_dir}/working.json. If schema_version field is missing, set schema_version='1.0' (in-place migration). Add turn to turns array. If turns array length > 10, compress oldest turns beyond 10 into summary field (extract key topics and decisions, append to existing summary). Update last_updated timestamp. WRITE SAFETY: write to working.json.tmp, rename to working.json, backup to working.json.bak.
SaveTurn.step3_7: ENTITY TRIPLE EXTRACTION (B2 v1.10.0 — Mem0 arXiv 2504.19413: graph-based memory, 26% accuracy gain, 90% token savings): After saving the turn to episodic storage, extract entity-relationship triples from the conversation turn. EXTRACTION: scan user_message and ai_response for entity mentions (people, places, topics, preferences, events). For each co-occurring entity pair, generate triple: { subject: string, relation: string, object: string, confidence: float(0-1), source_turn: turn_number, timestamp: now }. RELATION TYPES: 'likes', 'dislikes', 'knows', 'works_at', 'lives_in', 'interested_in', 'friend_of', 'family_of', 'worried_about', 'achieved', 'learning'. CONSOLIDATION: load existing relationships.json from {memory_dir}/semantic/{user_id}/relationships.json. For each new triple: (a) if matching subject+relation+object exists with confidence >= 0.5, update confidence using EWA: new_conf = alpha * extracted_conf + (1-alpha) * existing_conf, where alpha = 0.3. (b) if no match, append new triple. (c) if contradictory triple exists (same subject+relation, different object), keep both with recency flag, mark older as superseded=true. DEDUP: triples with token_overlap(subject+object, existing.subject+existing.object) > 0.8 (token_overlap algorithm: split both strings into word tokens, compute |intersection|/|union|) are considered matches — update rather than append. Write updated relationships.json. BUDGET: extraction is lightweight (pattern matching, not LLM inference). Skip if token_context.budget_remaining < 10%.
SaveTurn.step4: Return confirmation: { saved: true, turn_id, episodic_file: session_id, working_turns_count: number, summary_updated: boolean }.
SaveTurn.step4_5: KNOWLEDGE ITEM EXTRACTION (B1 v1.9.0 — spaced repetition knowledge capture, LECTOR 2025): After saving turn, scan the conversation turn for reviewable knowledge. DETECTION: a knowledge item exists when: (a) user learns a new concept and demonstrates understanding (not just passive reception), (b) user synthesizes information across sources, (c) AI teaches a thinking framework (from AdviceMode.step4_5 B3 v1.9.0) and user applies it, (d) user discovers an insight through Socratic dialogue. EXTRACTION: for each detected item, compose: { topic: string (max 50 chars), key_points: [string] (2-5 key points the user demonstrated understanding of), first_seen: current_timestamp, next_review: current_date + 1 day (initial interval), interval_days: 1, ease_factor: 2.5, review_count: 0 }. WRITE: read {memory_dir}/semantic/{user_id}/knowledge_items.json. If not found, initialize { items: [] }. Append new items. DEDUP ALGORITHM: normalize candidate topic (lowercase, collapse whitespace, remove stop words) → compute token overlap with each existing item.topic: overlap_ratio = |intersection(candidate_tokens, existing_tokens)| / |union(candidate_tokens, existing_tokens)|. If overlap_ratio >= 0.7: merge — combine key_points (deduplicate by substring containment), preserve earliest first_seen, set next_review = min(both), set interval_days = max(both). If overlap_ratio 0.4-0.7: create separate item with related_to: existing_item.topic for future manual consolidation. If overlap_ratio < 0.4: new distinct item. If dedup comparison fails (unexpected format), skip dedup for this item, log duplicate_risk=true, and proceed. Write with atomic .tmp+rename pattern. FREQUENCY: max 3 knowledge items extracted per session to prevent over-tracking.
SaveTurn.constraints: Both writes (episodic and working) must succeed. If episodic write succeeds but working write fails, retry working write — do not rollback episodic. Atomic write pattern: .tmp then rename for every write. .bak backup for every overwrite. Never lose user conversation data. FILE LOCKING STRATEGY: all file writes use advisory file locking (create {filename}.lock before write, remove after). Lock acquisition timeout: 5 seconds. If lock cannot be acquired, retry once after 1s delay, then fail with 'concurrent_write_conflict' error. This complements optimistic locking with pessimistic prevention for high-contention scenarios. UNIFIED LOCKING STRATEGY: Optimistic locking (timestamp comparison) is the PRIMARY concurrency control. File locking (.lock files) is SECONDARY, providing multi-process safety. Execution order: (1) acquire file lock, (2) perform optimistic lock check (compare timestamps), (3) if optimistic check fails: re-read file under lock → merge changes → write, (4) if optimistic check passes: write directly, (5) release file lock. This ensures both single-process and multi-process consistency.
SaveTurn.Error: Write fails -> retry once with explicit directory creation via file_system -> circuit-breaker (3 failures in 60s) -> fallback: return turn_data as JSON string for caller to cache in-memory, flag persistence failure -> inform caller to retry save_turn later.

TopicGraph.step1: TOPIC EXTRACTION: from turn_data (just saved by SaveTurn), extract discussed topics using keyword and semantic analysis. Each topic = { topic_id: string (normalized lowercase), mentions: number, first_seen: timestamp, last_seen: timestamp, sessions: [session_id], sentiment_avg: float(-1 to 1) }.
TopicGraph.step2: GRAPH UPDATE: read {memory_dir}/semantic/{user_id}/topic_graph.json via file_system. If not found, initialize: { schema_version: '3.0', user_id, topics: {}, arcs: [], temporal_edges: [], updated_at: now }. For each extracted topic: if exists in graph, update mentions count, last_seen, append session_id if new, update sentiment_avg with EMA (alpha=0.3). If new topic, add to graph. TOPIC ARC DETECTION: if current turn transitions from topic A to topic B (detected via topic shift in consecutive turns), record arc: { from: topic_A_id, to: topic_B_id, transition_count: increment, last_transition: now, transition_type: 'natural'|'user_initiated'|'ai_suggested', weight: float }. WEIGHTED EDGES WITH EXPONENTIAL DECAY (B4 v1.7.0 — Memoria framework arXiv 2512.12686 inspired, 87.1% accuracy, 38.7% latency reduction): All arcs now carry a weight field (float, 0.0-1.0) representing relationship strength. WEIGHT UPDATE: on each transition, update arc weight using Exponential Weighted Average (EWA): new_weight = alpha * 1.0 + (1 - alpha) * old_weight, where alpha = (threshold_config.topic_graph_ewa_alpha ?? 0.3). WEIGHT DECAY: on each graph access, apply time-based decay to ALL arcs: decayed_weight = weight * exp(-(threshold_config.edge_decay_lambda ?? 0.02) * days_since_last_transition). Arcs with decayed_weight < (threshold_config.edge_min_weight ?? 0.05) are pruned (weak associations naturally forgotten). This enables stronger recent associations to dominate while old unused connections gracefully fade. PRUNING PRIORITY: weight-based pruning replaces simple FIFO — lowest weight arcs are pruned first when capacity exceeded. TEMPORAL ENTITY RELATIONSHIPS (B5 v1.6.0 — Graphiti/Zep temporal KG-inspired): For entity relationships extracted by ConsolidateMemory (stored in relationships.json), maintain temporal edges in topic_graph. When a relationship changes (e.g., user changes job, moves city), record temporal_edge: { entity_id: string, relation: string, old_value: string|null, new_value: string, valid_from: ISO8601, valid_until: ISO8601|null (null=current), change_source: 'user_stated'|'inferred', session_id: string, weight: float (initial 1.0, decays per edge_decay_lambda) }. This enables temporal queries: 'Where did user work BEFORE current job?' Query support: filter temporal_edges by entity_id + relation, sort by valid_from descending. CAPACITY: max 500 temporal_edges per user, weight-based eviction of lowest-weight fully-superseded edges (where valid_until is not null). INTEGRATION: RecallMemory can query temporal_edges to provide time-aware answers, preferring higher-weight edges. ConsolidateMemory populates temporal_edges when relationship updates are detected (old_value != new_value in relationships.json merge).
TopicGraph.step3: CROSS-SESSION CONTINUITY: identify topic arcs that span multiple sessions (same topic discussed across 2+ different session_ids). Mark these as persistent_topics with cross_session_count. These are high-value topics for proactive recall — user keeps returning to them.
TopicGraph.step3_5: GROWTH MILESTONE TRACKING (C3 v1.8.0 — cross-session growth continuity): When processing turn_data, check for growth_signals from profiler. DATA FLOW: main.PostProcess step2 delegates to profiler with operation='analyze_turn'. Profiler returns growth_signals in response. Main.PostProcess step1 delegates to memory with operation='save_turn', passing growth_signals as part of turn_data.growth_signals field (null when profiler extraction fails or no signals detected). If growth_signals present: (a) Read growth_milestones section from topic_graph.json (initialize as { milestones: [], dimension_history: {} } if absent). (b) For each growth signal with signal_strength >= 0.6, record in dimension_history: { dimension: string, session_id, timestamp, signal_strength, direction, evidence }. Cap per-dimension history at 50 entries (FIFO). (c) MILESTONE DETECTION: compare current dimension level (rolling average of last 10 signals) against previous milestone level. If improvement >= 0.15 sustained over 3+ sessions, record milestone: { milestone_id: auto, dimension, old_level: float, new_level: float, achieved_at: timestamp, sessions_to_achieve: number, evidence_summary: string }. (d) CONTINUITY: on session start, LoadContext (operation='load_context') includes recent growth_milestones (last 3) in the returned context object as context.growth_milestones array. Main passes this to conversation module via context_handoff.memory_context.growth_milestones. Conversation module can reference these for continuity prompting ('Last time we worked on X, you showed improvement in Y'). Cap total milestones at 100 (prune oldest). Return growth_milestones_updated: boolean in output.
TopicGraph.step4: WRITE SAFETY: write updated topic_graph.json via atomic .tmp + rename, .bak backup. Return: { topics_updated: number, new_topics: number, arcs_updated: number, persistent_topics: [topic_id], growth_milestones_updated: boolean }.
TopicGraph.constraints: Lightweight per-turn operation — do not do deep semantic analysis, use keyword-based topic matching. DYNAMIC CAPACITY (B4): topic_graph base capacity = (threshold_config.topic_graph_base_capacity ?? 200). Growth: +((threshold_config.topic_graph_growth_rate ?? 50)) topics per (threshold_config.topic_graph_growth_interval ?? 100) conversation turns, capped at (threshold_config.topic_graph_max_capacity ?? 2000). Current capacity = min(base + floor(total_user_turns / growth_interval) * growth_rate, max_capacity). Cap topic_graph at current capacity (prune when topic count exceeds current dynamic capacity). Cap arcs at 1000 entries. Topic normalization: lowercase, strip common words, merge synonyms (e.g., 'cooking'/'recipes'/'food preparation' → 'cooking'). PRUNING ALGORITHM: when topic count exceeds current dynamic capacity (computed in DYNAMIC CAPACITY formula above), compute composite_score for each topic = mention_count * 0.4 + (1 / days_since_last_seen) * 0.3 + avg_sentiment_intensity * 0.3. Remove lowest 10% of topics (minimum 10) by composite_score. PROTECTED TOPICS: topics with at least one mention in the last 5 sessions are never pruned, regardless of score. HIERARCHICAL ORGANIZATION: Topics are organized in a parent-child hierarchy (category > subcategory > specific topic). When pruning, if a specific topic's importance drops below 0.15, merge it into its parent category rather than deleting. This preserves thematic knowledge while reducing node count.
TopicGraph.Error: Graph update fails -> retry -> circuit-breaker -> fallback: skip topic graph update for this turn, log warning -> inform caller.

RecallMemory.step1: Parse query into search terms. Extract key entities (names, dates, topics, events). Normalize: lowercase, remove stop words, expand abbreviations.
RecallMemory.step2: EPISODIC SEARCH: List files in {memory_dir}/episodic/ via file_system. For each session file, read and search turns for matches in user_input and agent_response fields. SEMANTIC MATCHING: beyond exact keyword matching, identify semantic equivalents — EN: 'mom' matches 'mother', 'happy' matches 'joy/excited'; ZH: '妈妈' matches '母亲', '开心' matches '高兴/快乐'; topic-level matches (asking about 'cooking'/'做菜' finds turns mentioning specific recipes). Score each match: (0.5 * semantic_relevance + 0.5 * term_frequency) * recency_bonus (newer sessions score higher). Collect top-10 candidate turns across all sessions.
RecallMemory.step2_5: ENHANCED SEMANTIC MATCHING: Beyond keyword matching, apply tf-idf-inspired scoring: (a) Term frequency: boost matches where query terms appear multiple times in a memory entry. (b) Inverse document frequency: boost matches on rare terms (appearing in <10% of entries) over common terms. (c) Combine with existing relevance scoring (0.4*semantic + 0.3*recency + 0.2*emotional + 0.1*entity) by adding tf_idf_boost * 0.15 (redistributed from semantic weight: 0.25*semantic + 0.15*tf_idf + 0.3*recency + 0.2*emotional + 0.1*entity). ENTITY CO-OCCURRENCE BOOST (B3): Read {memory_dir}/semantic/{user_id}/relationships.json. For each query entity, find co-occurring entities from the relationship graph (entities connected by any relation). If a memory entry mentions a co-occurring entity (even without the query entity), boost its relevance score by +0.1 (entity_graph_boost). This enables associative recall — asking about 'cooking' finds entries about 'kitchen renovation' if the relationship graph links them. Cap entity_graph_boost at +0.2 per entry. ENTITY GRAPH BOOST (B2 v1.10.0): after computing base relevance scores, load {memory_dir}/semantic/{user_id}/relationships.json. For each recalled memory, if it mentions entities that appear in the relationship graph with confidence >= 0.5, apply +0.1 relevance boost per matched entity (max +0.3 total). This leverages the entity triples extracted by SaveTurn.step3_7 to improve associative recall quality.
RecallMemory.step3: SEMANTIC SEARCH: Read {memory_dir}/semantic/facts.json and {memory_dir}/semantic/preferences.json. Search for query term matches in fact content and preference descriptions. Score: exact match > partial match > related topic match.
RecallMemory.step4: MERGE AND RANK: Combine episodic and semantic results. Final score = 0.4 * relevance_score + 0.35 * recency_score + 0.25 * source_quality (semantic facts score higher quality than raw episodic). Return top-K results (K=5 default): { results: [{ source: 'episodic'|'semantic', content: string, session_id?: string, timestamp?: string, relevance_score: number }], total_searched: number, query_terms: [...] }.
RecallMemory.constraints: Read-only operation. Limit episodic file reads to most recent 50 sessions for performance. If >50 session files exist, prioritize by file modification date (newest first). Never return raw internal scoring data to end user — only formatted content with source attribution.
RecallMemory.Error: Search fails -> retry once -> circuit-breaker (3 failures in 60s) -> fallback: return empty results with message 'Memory search temporarily unavailable' -> inform caller.

ConsolidateMemory.step1: List files in {memory_dir}/episodic/ via file_system. Identify sessions older than consolidation threshold (default: 7 days since last turn timestamp). Skip sessions already marked as consolidated (check metadata field 'consolidated: true').
ConsolidateMemory.step2: For each qualifying session, read full conversation log. Extract durable facts: (a) User-stated biographical facts (name, age, occupation, location, family). (b) Stated opinions and preferences (likes, dislikes, communication style). (c) Recurring topics (mentioned in 2+ sessions). (d) Relationship milestones (first meeting, shared experiences, established rapport). (e) Explicit requests to remember something. Classify each extraction with confidence: HIGH (user explicitly stated) / MEDIUM (inferred from context) / LOW (single mention, tentative).
ConsolidateMemory.step2b: ENTITY RELATIONSHIP EXTRACTION (Mem0g-inspired, 68.4% accuracy improvement): From each qualifying session's conversation log, extract entity relationships as structured tuples: { subject: string, relation: string, object: string, confidence: float(0-1) }. Examples: {subject: 'user', relation: 'prefers', object: 'concise_answers', confidence: 0.85}, {subject: 'user', relation: 'works_at', object: 'company_name', confidence: 0.90}, {subject: 'user', relation: 'lives_in', object: 'city_name', confidence: 0.95}. Subjects and objects are normalized to lowercase identifiers. Relations use a controlled vocabulary: prefers, works_at, lives_in, knows, studies, likes, dislikes, owns, uses, related_to. Read {memory_dir}/semantic/{user_id}/relationships.json via file_system. If not found, initialize: { schema_version: '1.0', relationships: [] } and create {memory_dir}/semantic/{user_id}/ directory if not exists. MERGE STRATEGY: for each extracted relationship, check if an existing relationship with same subject+relation pair exists. If yes, update the object and confidence with the newer extraction (prefer_newer), preserve the old value in a history array: { old_object, replaced_at }. If no existing match, append as new relationship. WRITE SAFETY: atomic .tmp + rename, .bak backup. This creates a graph-like semantic memory structure enabling richer contextual retrieval. SEMANTIC INDEX GENERATION (B3): After relationship extraction, generate {memory_dir}/semantic/{user_id}/semantic_index.json: { schema_version: '1.0', clusters: [{ cluster_id: string, theme: string, session_ids: [string], entity_ids: [string], centroid_topics: [string] }], updated_at: timestamp }. Clusters are formed by grouping sessions that share 3+ common entities or 2+ common topics. This index accelerates RecallMemory semantic search by providing pre-computed topic clusters for associative recall. WRITE SAFETY: atomic .tmp + rename, .bak backup. MERGE STRATEGY DIVERGENCE NOTE: Relationships use prefer_newer strategy (not three-way merge) because relationships are binary assertions ({subject, relation, object}) where only the latest extraction is relevant. This is intentional divergence from the fact merge strategy — facts are multi-source and benefit from three-way resolution; relationships are single-source updates.
ConsolidateMemory.step3: MERGE FACTS: Read {memory_dir}/semantic/facts.json. For each extracted fact: if it updates an existing fact (same topic/entity), CONFLICT DETECTION — if new fact contradicts existing (e.g., 'lives in NYC' vs stored 'lives in LA'), mark conflict: { old_value, new_value, conflict_detected_at, resolution: 'prefer_newer' }. THREE-WAY MERGE: when conflict detected, attempt three-way resolution chain: (1) ORIGINAL — check the earliest recorded value for this fact (from fact.history if available). (2) SEMANTIC — check semantic/preferences.json for corroborating data. (3) EPISODIC — check recent episodic entries for latest user statement. Resolution priority: if user explicitly stated new value in recent episodic turn (within 7 days), prefer episodic (user's latest word). If semantic preferences corroborate either value, prefer corroborated. If no resolution, prefer_newer with conflict_note for user verification at next profile query. Log merge_chain: [original_value, semantic_value, episodic_value, resolution_chosen]. Log conflict for transparency. Merge — keep higher confidence, update timestamp, preserve history including conflict record. If new fact, append. WRITE SAFETY: atomic .tmp + rename, .bak backup. Write updated facts.json.
ConsolidateMemory.step4: MERGE PREFERENCES: Read {memory_dir}/semantic/preferences.json. Same merge logic as facts. Categories: communication (formal/casual, emoji usage, response length), topics (favorite subjects, avoid topics), emotional (comfort style, humor appreciation), practical (scheduling, reminders). WRITE SAFETY: atomic .tmp + rename, .bak backup. Write updated preferences.json.
ConsolidateMemory.step4c: HIERARCHICAL SUMMARIZATION (progressive compression — research-backed: Recursive Summarization, Context Rot mitigation): Apply three-level compression based on session age: LEVEL 1 — RECENT (sessions < 7 days old): keep individual turn summaries at near-verbatim detail (full user_input and agent_response preserved, only strip filler words and redundant pleasantries). LEVEL 2 — OLDER (sessions 7-30 days old): compress to session-level summaries (one summary paragraph per session covering: main topics discussed, key decisions, emotional tone, unresolved items; individual turns are replaced by the session summary). LEVEL 3 — ANCIENT (sessions 30+ days old): compress to topic-level facts only (extract atomic fact statements, discard conversational context and narrative flow; only durable knowledge survives — e.g., 'user prefers morning meetings', 'user is allergic to shellfish'). For each processed session, apply the appropriate compression level based on the age of its last turn timestamp relative to current date. Write compressed versions back to episodic files with metadata { compression_level: 1|2|3, compressed_at: now }. WRITE SAFETY: atomic .tmp + rename, .bak backup.
ConsolidateMemory.step5: Mark consolidated sessions: update each processed episodic file with metadata { consolidated: true, consolidated_at: now, compression_level: 1|2|3 }. WRITE SAFETY: atomic write.
ConsolidateMemory.step6: Return summary: { sessions_processed: number, facts_extracted: number, facts_merged: number, preferences_extracted: number, preferences_merged: number, relationships_extracted: number, relationships_merged: number, compression_applied: { level1_count: number, level2_count: number, level3_count: number } }.
ConsolidateMemory.constraints: Never delete episodic data during consolidation — only mark as consolidated. LOW confidence extractions are stored but flagged for verification at next user interaction. Consolidation is idempotent — re-running on already-consolidated sessions is a no-op. TIER INTEGRATION CLARIFICATION: Three-way merge (step3) resolves FACT conflicts in the semantic memory tier. Hierarchical summarization (step4c) compresses old SESSION records in the episodic memory tier. These operate on different data tiers and are NOT redundant. conflict_note produced by merge persists in semantic memory until explicit user acknowledgment or next consolidation cycle.
ConsolidateMemory.Error: Extraction fails for one session -> skip that session, continue others -> log skipped session_id. Write fails -> retry once -> circuit-breaker (3 failures in 60s) -> fallback: return partial results with list of failed sessions -> inform caller.

DecayPrune.step0_5: DATA RETENTION TTL CHECK (B3): Before scoring, enforce category-specific retention policies. TTL CATEGORIES: (a) WORKING — tier: session-scoped, TTL: session end (auto-purged when session closes, no decay needed). (b) EPISODIC — tier: conversation logs, TTL: 90 days from last_turn_at. Sessions older than 90 days AND marked consolidated → compress to summary-only (step5). Sessions older than 90 days AND NOT consolidated → force-consolidate first, then compress. (c) SEMANTIC — tier: extracted knowledge (facts.json, preferences.json), TTL: permanent (user-deletable via DeleteData). Semantic items are never auto-deleted by TTL — only by explicit user request or decay scoring below archive threshold. (d) PROFILES — tier: user profile data, TTL: permanent (user-deletable via DeleteData). Profile data persists indefinitely unless user requests deletion. (e) AUDIT — tier: safety audit logs, TTL: 365 days (enforced by SafetyAuditLog.step4). TTL enforcement runs BEFORE importance scoring to ensure expired episodic sessions are processed first. LOG: ttl_actions: { episodic_expired: number, episodic_force_consolidated: number }.
DecayPrune.step1: Read {memory_dir}/semantic/facts.json and {memory_dir}/semantic/preferences.json via file_system. For each memory item, calculate importance score: importance(t) = recency_weight * e^(-lambda * age_days) + access_weight * log(1 + access_count) + base_importance. Default weights: recency_weight=0.5, lambda=(threshold_config.decay_lambda ?? 0.01), access_weight=0.3, base_importance=0.2.
DecayPrune.step2: Classify by score: (a) RETAIN (score >= 0.4) — no action, keep as-is. (b) DEMOTE (0.15 <= score < 0.4) — mark as 'low_priority', reduce detail level in context assembly. (c) ARCHIVE (score < 0.15) — compress to one-line summary, move to archived section within same file. Never auto-delete.
DecayPrune.step3: PROTECTED ITEMS: Never decay or archive: (a) User-stated identity facts (name, birthday, family). (b) Items with confidence HIGH. (c) Items explicitly requested to be remembered ('please remember...'). (d) Items accessed in last 30 days regardless of age. Protected items keep minimum score of 0.5.
DecayPrune.step4: Apply decay decisions. Update facts.json and preferences.json with new scores, statuses, and archived entries. WRITE SAFETY: atomic .tmp + rename, .bak backup for each file.
DecayPrune.step5: EPISODIC CLEANUP: For episodic sessions older than 90 days AND marked consolidated, compress full turns to summary-only (keep first turn, last turn, and summary — remove intermediate turns). WRITE SAFETY: atomic write. Never delete session files. TTL INTEGRATION (B3): step0_5 may have force-consolidated additional expired sessions — step5 processes all consolidated sessions uniformly regardless of how they became consolidated.
DecayPrune.step6: Return report: { total_items_scored: number, retained: number, demoted: number, archived: number, protected: number, episodic_compressed: number, ttl_actions: { episodic_expired: number, episodic_force_consolidated: number } }.
DecayPrune.constraints: Never auto-delete any user data without explicit consent. Archive means compress and deprioritize, not remove. Decay runs are idempotent. Score calculation must be deterministic given same inputs. All writes atomic with backup. DATA RETENTION TTL (B3): TTL categories define maximum retention periods per data tier. Working memory is session-volatile. Episodic sessions compress after 90 days (never deleted). Semantic and profile data persist until user-initiated deletion. Audit logs follow 365-day retention with archival. EDPB COMPLIANCE: these retention periods are explicit and documented per EDPB guidelines — no implicit indefinite retention.
DecayPrune.Error: Scoring fails -> retry once -> circuit-breaker (3 failures in 60s) -> fallback: skip decay cycle, return error report with reason -> inform caller. Partial write failure -> do not apply remaining changes, report which files succeeded and which failed.

ExportData.step1: Compile all user data across tiers. Read: (a) {memory_dir}/profiles/{user_id}.json — user profile. (b) {memory_dir}/episodic/*.json — all session logs where user_id matches. (c) {memory_dir}/semantic/facts.json — filter facts attributed to user_id. (d) {memory_dir}/semantic/preferences.json — filter preferences for user_id. (e) {memory_dir}/working.json — current session state if user_id matches.
ExportData.step2: Assemble portable export object: { format_version: '1.1.0', exported_at: ISO timestamp, user_id: string, profile: {...}, episodic_sessions: [{session_id, turns, created_at, last_turn_at}...], semantic_facts: [...], semantic_preferences: [...], topic_graph: read {memory_dir}/semantic/{user_id}/topic_graph.json and include full topic graph data, working_memory: {...}|null, metadata: { total_sessions: number, total_turns: number, total_facts: number, date_range: {earliest, latest} }, README: { format_description: 'SoulBot Chat memory export — contains conversation history, learned preferences, topic relationships, and behavioral patterns. All data is in JSON format and can be inspected with any text editor.', sections: ['profile', 'episodic_sessions', 'semantic_facts', 'semantic_preferences', 'topic_graph', 'working_memory'], privacy_note: 'This export contains personal data. Handle with care.' } }. DYNAMIC FORMAT VERSION: format_version is derived from program version major.minor (e.g., program v1.2.1 → format_version '1.2'). BACKWARD COMPATIBILITY: if export consumer declares a target format_version older than current, exclude fields added after that version. Fields by version: v1.0=base fields, v1.2=topic_graph+README. Always include format_version in export metadata for consumer compatibility detection.
ExportData.step3: Write export to {memory_dir}/exports/{user_id}_export_{timestamp}.json via file_system. Create exports/ directory if not exists. WRITE SAFETY: atomic .tmp + rename.
ExportData.step4: Return: { export_file: string (path), size_bytes: number (estimated), contents_summary: { sessions: number, turns: number, facts: number, preferences: number } }.
ExportData.constraints: Include ALL user data — nothing omitted. Export format must be self-contained and human-readable JSON. Do not include internal scoring metadata or system-only fields (decay scores, consolidation flags). Sanitize: remove any references to other users' data if present in shared sessions.
ExportData.Error: Read fails for one tier -> continue with available tiers, note missing sections in export metadata -> retry failed reads once -> circuit-breaker -> fallback: partial export with clear inventory of what is included and what is missing -> inform caller.

DeleteData.step1: CONFIRMATION GATE: This operation requires explicit confirmation from the caller. If no confirmation flag is set (confirmed: true in turn_data or explicit caller signal), return { requires_confirmation: true, message: 'Data deletion is irreversible. Confirm to proceed.', data_inventory: {sessions: N, facts: N, preferences: N, profile: exists/missing} }. Do NOT proceed without confirmation. CONFIRMATION SPECIFICATION: confirmed flag must be boolean true in turn_data.confirmed OR the user's message in the current turn must contain explicit deletion confirmation text ('确认删除', 'confirm delete', 'yes delete'). NO TIMEOUT AUTO-PROCEED: if no confirmation received in the current turn, abort deletion and inform user that confirmation is required. Re-confirmation after backup is NOT required — the single confirmation gate is sufficient.
DeleteData.step2: PRE-DELETE BACKUP: Before any deletion, trigger ExportData operation internally to create a full backup export at {memory_dir}/exports/{user_id}_pre_delete_{timestamp}.json. WRITE SAFETY: atomic write. If backup fails, ABORT deletion — inform caller backup must succeed before deletion can proceed.
DeleteData.step3: DELETE PHASE — execute in order: (a) Delete {memory_dir}/episodic/{session_id}.json for all sessions matching user_id. Also delete corresponding .tmp and .bak files. (b) Remove user entries from {memory_dir}/semantic/facts.json and {memory_dir}/semantic/preferences.json — read, filter out user_id entries, write back. WRITE SAFETY: atomic. (b2) Delete {memory_dir}/semantic/{user_id}/ directory and all contents (topic_graph.json, relationships.json, and any .tmp/.bak files). This ensures GDPR Article 17 compliance for all user-scoped semantic data introduced by TopicGraph and entity relationship extraction. (c) Delete {memory_dir}/profiles/{user_id}.json and its .tmp/.bak files. (d) If {memory_dir}/working.json belongs to user_id, reset to empty state.
DeleteData.step4: AUDIT TRAIL: Read {memory_dir}/audit/deletion_log.json via file_system. If not found, initialize: { schema_version: '1.0', entries: [] }. Create audit/ directory if not exists. Append entry: { user_id, deleted_at: ISO timestamp, items_deleted: { sessions: number, facts: number, preferences: number, topic_graph: boolean, relationships: boolean, profile: boolean, working: boolean }, backup_file: string (export path) }. WRITE SAFETY: atomic .tmp + rename.
DeleteData.step5: Return: { deleted: true, user_id, items_deleted: {...counts}, backup_file: string, audit_logged: true }.
DeleteData.constraints: IRREVERSIBLE after completion — hence mandatory backup before delete. Confirmation is non-negotiable — never skip step 1. Audit log must survive deletion (it contains no user content, only metadata). If any deletion step fails mid-way, report partial deletion state — do not silently continue. Caller must be informed which items were deleted and which remain.
DeleteData.Error: Backup fails -> ABORT, do not delete anything -> inform caller. Partial deletion failure -> halt remaining deletions -> return partial report with what was deleted and what remains -> retry remaining items once -> circuit-breaker -> fallback: return detailed error state for manual intervention -> inform caller.

ProactiveRecall.step1: INJECTION PROTOCOL: ProactiveRecall produces ranked list by priority: (1) upcoming_events, (2) unresolved_topics, (3) follow_ups. Main passes top-3 candidates to conversation. Conversation selects max 1 for injection (highest relevance_score). Unused candidates discarded per-turn. FOLLOW-UP SEARCH: Read {memory_dir}/semantic/facts.json and {memory_dir}/semantic/preferences.json. Identify items tagged with temporal markers (dates, deadlines, EN: 'next week', 'tomorrow', 'coming up'; ZH: '下周', '明天', '快到了', '马上要'; upcoming events). Check current date against event dates — collect items due within 7 days as upcoming_events: [{ event, date, reminder }].
ProactiveRecall.step2: UNRESOLVED TOPICS: Read {memory_dir}/working.json. Scan recent turns for questions asked but not fully answered, topics interrupted by digression, pending slot fills from previous sessions. Collect unresolved_topics: [{ topic, turns_since_mentioned }]. Limit to 3 most recent.
ProactiveRecall.step3: FOLLOW-UP OPPORTUNITIES: Read recent {memory_dir}/episodic/ sessions. Identify topics where user expressed ongoing interest or shared plans ('I am going to try that recipe', 'I have an interview next week'). Cross-reference with semantic facts for relevance. Collect follow_ups: [{ topic, last_mentioned, suggested_prompt }]. Limit to 2 most relevant.
ProactiveRecall.step4: Return proactive_context: { follow_ups, upcoming_events, unresolved_topics }. If no proactive items found, return empty arrays.
ProactiveRecall.constraints: Read-only operation. Proactive items must be genuinely relevant — do not fabricate connections. Limit total proactive items to 5 to avoid overwhelming the conversation. Temporal calculations use current date. Only suggest follow-ups for topics mentioned in last 14 days.
ProactiveRecall.Error: Search fails -> retry -> circuit-breaker -> fallback: return empty proactive_context -> inform caller.

SaveSessionSummary.step1: Receive session_data: { turns, topics_discussed, emotional_arc, key_moments }. Generate concise session summary: 2-3 sentences covering main topics, emotional trajectory, and notable interactions (corrections, milestones, new facts learned).
SaveSessionSummary.step2: Read {memory_dir}/episodic/{session_id}.json. Append session_summary field: { summary_text, topics: [], emotional_arc: string, key_facts_learned: [], duration_turns: number, generated_at: timestamp }. WRITE SAFETY: atomic .tmp + rename, .bak backup.
SaveSessionSummary.step3: Update {memory_dir}/working.json — set session_ended: true, session_summary: summary_text. This marks the session for consolidation eligibility.
SaveSessionSummary.step4: Return: { summary_generated: true, summary_text, session_id, topics_count, facts_learned_count }.
SaveSessionSummary.constraints: Summary must be factual — only include what actually happened in the session. Do not embellish or interpret. Keep summary under 500 characters. If session had fewer than 3 turns, generate minimal summary.
SaveSessionSummary.Error: Summary generation fails -> retry -> circuit-breaker -> fallback: save raw session metadata without narrative summary -> inform caller.

ImportData.description: Import previously exported user data with format validation, conflict resolution, and integrity verification. Counterpart to ExportData for full GDPR data portability compliance.
ImportData.step1: FORMAT VALIDATION: Verify import file structure matches expected export format. Check format_version field: (a) If format_version matches current (v1.2), proceed directly. (b) If format_version is older, apply migration transforms (v1.0→v1.1: add topic_graph field; v1.1→v1.2: add entity_relationships). (c) If format_version is newer than current, REJECT with error 'Import format version {v} is newer than supported {current}. Please update SoulBot.'
ImportData.step2: INTEGRITY CHECK: Verify data consistency: (a) All required fields present (user_id, episodic_entries, semantic_entries). (b) Timestamps are valid ISO 8601. (c) No circular entity relationships. (d) Profile schema compatible with current version. (e) user_id in import matches current user_id OR user explicitly confirms cross-user import.
ImportData.step3: CONFLICT RESOLUTION: For each data category, apply merge strategy: (a) Episodic memories: APPEND (no conflicts possible — each entry is unique by session_id+timestamp). (b) Semantic facts: THREE-WAY MERGE — if existing fact contradicts imported fact, keep both with conflict_note and lower confidence (max 0.6). (c) Entity relationships: PREFER_NEWER by timestamp. (d) Topic graph: UNION merge — combine topics, merge duplicate nodes by name similarity > 0.8 (token_overlap algorithm: split into word tokens, compute |intersection|/|union|). (e) Profile data: PREFER_EXISTING for learned fields, PREFER_IMPORTED for user-stated fields.
ImportData.step4: APPLY IMPORT: Write merged data using atomic write pattern (.tmp + rename). Create pre-import backup of all affected files with suffix .pre_import.bak. Log import summary: {entries_added, entries_merged, conflicts_found, conflicts_resolved}.
ImportData.step5: USER CONFIRMATION: Present import summary to user before finalizing: '{N} memories imported, {M} merged with existing data, {C} conflicts resolved. Your data has been restored.' If user did not pre-confirm, require explicit confirmation before step4.
ImportData.output: Import result: {success: boolean, entries_imported, conflicts, backup_path}
ImportData.constraints: (A) NEVER overwrite existing data without backup. (B) NEVER import data for a different user without explicit confirmation. (C) Maximum import size: 10MB. (D) Import is NOT idempotent — running twice will duplicate episodic entries. Guard with import_id deduplication.
ImportData.Error: retry_count: 1, window_seconds: 60. Rollback: restore from .pre_import.bak files. Log import_failed with error details.

MemoryStats.description: Provide memory usage statistics and health metrics. Helps users understand their data footprint and memory system health.
MemoryStats.step1: INVENTORY: Count entries across all memory tiers: (a) Working memory: current session turns, context size in estimated tokens. (b) Episodic: total sessions, total turns, date range (oldest to newest). (c) Semantic: total facts, total entity relationships, topic graph node count. (d) Profile: fields populated count, last updated timestamp, schema version.
MemoryStats.step2: STORAGE ESTIMATE: Calculate approximate storage: (a) Sum file sizes in memory/ directory tree. (b) Categorize by tier: episodic (typically largest), semantic, profile, working. (c) Format as human-readable: '2.3 MB total (1.8 MB conversations, 0.3 MB knowledge, 0.2 MB profile)'.
MemoryStats.step3: HEALTH METRICS: Assess memory health: (a) Staleness: % of semantic facts not accessed in 90+ days. (b) Conflict rate: % of facts with unresolved conflict_notes. (c) Coverage: % of sessions with successful summaries. (d) Decay candidates: count of items below retention threshold.
MemoryStats.step4: RECOMMENDATIONS: Based on health metrics, suggest actions: (a) If staleness > 30%: 'Consider running memory consolidation to archive old knowledge.' (b) If conflict_rate > 10%: 'You have {N} conflicting memories — review them to improve accuracy.' (c) If storage > 5MB: 'Your memory is growing large. Consider exporting and archiving older conversations.'
MemoryStats.output: Formatted statistics report with inventory, storage, health, and recommendations.
MemoryStats.constraints: (A) Read-only operation — never modify any files. (B) File size calculation is BEST EFFORT (handle permission errors gracefully). (C) Present in user's language per L1.
MemoryStats.Error: Statistics computation fails -> retry -> circuit-breaker -> fallback: return partial stats with data_incomplete=true -> inform.

SpacedRecall.description: Spaced repetition knowledge review engine. Identifies knowledge items due for review and generates retrieval-practice questions to strengthen long-term retention.
SpacedRecall.step1: LOAD KNOWLEDGE ITEMS: Read {memory_dir}/semantic/{user_id}/knowledge_items.json via file_system. If not found, return { review_due: false, items: [] } — no knowledge tracked yet. Schema: { items: [{ topic: string, key_points: [string], first_seen: ISO8601, next_review: ISO8601, interval_days: number, ease_factor: float (default 2.5), review_count: number, last_direction: 'correct'|'partial'|'forgot' }] }.
SpacedRecall.step2: FILTER DUE ITEMS: select items where next_review <= current_date. Sort by: (a) overdue duration descending (most overdue first), (b) ease_factor ascending (hardest items first). Select top 2 items for review (max 2 per session to prevent quiz fatigue).
SpacedRecall.step2_5: CONCEPT INTERFERENCE CHECK (B4 v1.10.0 — LECTOR arXiv 2508.03275: semantic-aware spaced repetition, 90.2% vs 88.4% baseline): Before presenting review items from SM-2 queue, check for semantic interference between scheduled items. INTERFERENCE DETECTION: for each pair of items due for review in the same session, compute semantic_similarity using token_overlap (same algorithm as SaveTurn.step3_7: split into word tokens, |intersection|/|union|). If token_overlap > 0.6 between any two items (confused pair), apply INTERLEAVING: schedule the less urgent item (longer SM-2 interval) to next session, keeping the more urgent item for current review. RATIONALE: reviewing semantically similar items in the same session causes interference effects (LECTOR finding). Separating confused pairs across sessions improves retention. LIMIT: maximum 3 items per review session (prevents cognitive overload). If more than 3 items are due, prioritize by SM-2 urgency (shortest interval first), apply interference check, present top 3. LOG: track interference_pairs_detected and items_deferred in review metadata for SpacedRecall analytics.
SpacedRecall.step3: GENERATE RETRIEVAL QUESTIONS: For each due item, generate a retrieval-practice question that requires active recall, NOT recognition. Pattern: 'Last time you learned about [topic] — you identified [N] key aspects. Can you recall what they were?' or 'You explored [topic] recently. What was the main insight you took away?' Questions must reference the topic but NOT reveal the key_points (user must recall them). TOPIC RELEVANCE: if current conversation has a natural connection to the due item's topic, weave the review into the conversation flow. If no connection, present as a brief 'knowledge check-in' at conversation start.
SpacedRecall.step4: RESPONSE EVALUATION + SM-2 UPDATE: After user responds to review question, evaluate recall quality: (a) CORRECT (user recalls 80%+ of key_points) → new_interval = interval * ease_factor, ease_factor = max(1.3, ease_factor + 0.1), review_count++. (b) PARTIAL (user recalls 40-79%) → new_interval = interval * 1.3, ease_factor unchanged, review_count++. (c) FORGOT (user recalls <40%) → new_interval = 1 (reset to 1 day), ease_factor = max(1.3, ease_factor - 0.2), review_count++. Update next_review = current_date + new_interval. Write updated knowledge_items.json. POSITIVE FRAMING: never say 'you forgot' — use 'let me help you refresh that' or 'here's a quick recap to strengthen that memory'.
SpacedRecall.output: { review_due: boolean, items_reviewed: number, recall_quality: [string], next_scheduled_reviews: [{ topic, next_review }] }
SpacedRecall.constraints: (A) Max 2 review items per session — prevent quiz fatigue. (B) Review questions must require ACTIVE RECALL, not multiple choice. (C) SM-2 algorithm parameters: initial interval = 1 day, initial ease_factor = 2.5, minimum ease = 1.3. (D) Never present reviews during crisis or emotional distress (check context). (E) Knowledge items are user-learned insights, NOT AI-taught facts — only items where user demonstrated understanding are tracked.
SpacedRecall.Error: Knowledge file read/write fails -> retry once -> circuit-breaker -> fallback: skip spaced recall for this session, return { review_due: false, error: 'storage_unavailable' } -> inform caller.
